FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study Release 3.0 data, N=5,166 participants aged 9-10 years at baseline, recruited from 21 U.S. sites (Volkow et al., 2018).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal linear mixed-effects models (LMMs), JIVE decomposition of brain co-development, and mediation analysis using ABCD baseline and two-year follow-up data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions; no indication of being a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: All results tables (Table A2, Fig. 2B mediation diagram, text) report only the composite 'baseline total screen time' (or 'SMA') variable — no separate per-modality coefficient for phone/SM-specific items (social media, texting, etc.) appears anywhere in main text, appendix, or supplementary. Step 2: Per the methods, the SMA composite explicitly includes 'watching TV shows/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting on an electronic device, visiting social networking sites, and video-chatting' — it bundles TV and video games with phone/SM items, and no separate breakout of phone/SM-only items is provided. The composite therefore includes non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV, video games) without a separate phone/SM-specific estimate, failing Step 2.

notes: SMA composite explicitly includes TV/movies, video games, texting, social networking sites, and video-chat; no per-modality breakout in any results location. Fails IC2 Step 2 due to non-phone/non-SM ingredients bundled without separate breakout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 2.0 baseline data, n=11,780 youth aged 9-10 years, multi-site U.S. sample (Volkow et al., 2018).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis: relative risk (RR) and adjusted relative risk (ARR) models examining associations between screen media activities and DSM-5 internalizing disorder diagnoses from K-SADS-5.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Journal of Affective Disorders; no indication of being a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports separate ARR estimates for social media use (girls: ARR=2.20, 95% CI 1.13-4.30 for depressive disorders; boys: ARR=2.94, 95% CI 1.59-5.42) and texting (boys: ARR=2.30, 95% CI 1.34-3.97 for depressive disorders) — phone/SM-specific variables paired with clinical DSM-5 internalizing disorder diagnoses (individual-trait mental health outcomes). Text also reports social media user vs. non-user comparisons with ARR for depressive disorders and suicidal ideation.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative ARR and 95% CI reported throughout Tables 2 and 3 for social media, texting, video chat, and other screen modalities against K-SADS-5 DSM-5 diagnoses.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed: general linear model with adjusted relative risk estimates for SM and texting against clinically-assessed internalizing disorder outcomes.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 does not apply. SM/phone variables appear as IV (predictors of internalizing disorder diagnoses).
notes: Clear Step 1 hit: Table 3 has separate ARR coefficients for social media use and texting (phone/SM-specific) paired with DSM-5 internalizing disorder diagnoses. Cross-sectional ABCD baseline data.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study data (NIMH Data Archive DOI 10.15154/1519007), 11,871 participants aged 9-10 years from 21 U.S. research sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a measurement/methods paper describing development of the ABCD screen use questionnaire, longitudinal descriptive changes in screen usage, and psychometric analyses (CFA, measurement invariance) of the SMAQ and VGAQ scales. All inferential analyses are psychometric model comparisons (chi-square, CFI, RMSEA, factor loadings) and descriptive sex/race mean comparisons on screen use and addiction scale scores. No external individual-trait outcome (health, mental health, cognitive, behavioral, neural) is analyzed. The paper is a measurement resource/instrument description, not an original empirical analysis examining associations with an outcome.

notes: Measurement/psychometric paper: describes screen use questionnaire development, SMAQ and VGAQ factor structure/invariance, and longitudinal descriptive screen use trends. No external health or behavioral outcome analyzed. Excluded under FT-EC3.
